text: The surgeon uses a vessel sealer energy device to take down the short gastric vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach. The stomach is medialized, and the posterior gastric attachments are dissected. The left crus of the diaphragm is approached, and the hernia sac is entered.
The surgeon begins dissection along the lesser curvature of the stomach, identifying the right crura of the diaphragm and performing blunt dissection to separate the hernia sac.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to extend the left-sided dissection into the mediastinum, identifying the left vagus nerve and continuing the mediastinal esophageal dissection. Posterior attachments are taken down, and the right-sided mediastinal dissection proceeds with caution to avoid the pleura and the right vagus nerve. Circumferential control of the esophagus is achieved, with careful dissection around the mediastinal aorta.
The surgeon resects the hernia sac. An endoscope is passed, and the stomach is insufflated to confirm the intra-abdominal location of the gastroesophageal (GE) junction.
The surgeon begins the primary posterior cruroplasty using interrupted Ethabon sutures, which are tied intercorporeally with robotic assistance.

A six by nine centimeter piece of alloderm mesh is cut into a keyhole configuration and placed in the abdomen. The mesh is unfurled and passed behind the esophagus. The keyhole portion is closed with Ethabon sutures, and the mesh is secured in place using four-point fixation.
